--- SOURCE 1: What are Entrepreneurial Ecosystems? ---
URL: https://www.kauffman.org/ecosystem-playbook-draft-3/ecosystems/

SUMMARY:
The essence of an entrepreneurial ecosystem is its people and the culture of trust and collaboration that allows them to interact successfully. – People and institutions with knowledge and resources to help entrepreneurs. – Stories that people tell about themselves and their ecosystem. We need diverse inputs for entrepreneurship – from the people who start companies and work for them to the resources they access – and we need a culture that supports heterogeneous communities, a range of industries, and a wealth of different ideas. Ecosystem building requires attracting, retaining, and cultivating talented people – including entrepreneurs, but also potential employers and other supporting partners. The collective story that people tell themselves – and the rest of the world – powerfully shapes an ecosystem’s future.

# What are Entrepreneurial Ecosystems?

## The rise of the rest

**Communities like Silicon Valley, Boston, Tel Aviv, London, Boulder, and Berlin took decades to become robust entrepreneurial ecosystems.[22](https://www.kauffman.org/ecosystem-playbook-draft-3/glossary-and-resources/endnotes)** However, such ecosystems can grow anywhere today. In the modern economy, every community has the opportunity to become a thriving ecosystem. In fact, many already have.

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/globe.png?resize=400%2C404)

## Defining an ecosystem

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/innovation_thrives_in_dense_networks.png?resize=756%2C433)

**The essence of an entrepreneurial ecosystem is its people and the culture of trust and collaboration that allows them to interact successfully.** An ecosystem that allows for the fast flow of talent, information, and resources helps entrepreneurs quickly find what they need at each stage of growth. As a result, the whole is greater than the sum of its separate parts.[23](https://www.kauffman.org/ecosystem-playbook-draft-3/glossary-and-resources/endnotes)

### Elements of an ecosystem.

A thriving ecosystem includes these key elements:

– **Entrepreneurs** who aspire to start and grow new businesses, and the people who support entrepreneurs.

– **Talent** that can help companies grow.

– People and institutions with **knowledge and resources** to help entrepreneurs.

– Individuals and institutions that serve as **champion and conveners** of entrepreneurs and the ecosystem.

– **Onramps** (or access points) to the ecosystem so that anyone and everyone can participate.

– **Intersections** that facilitate the interaction of people, ideas, and resources.

– **Stories** that people tell about themselves and their ecosystem.

– **Culture** that is rich in social capital – collaboration, cooperation, trust, reciprocity, and a focus on the common good – makes the ecosystem come alive by connecting all the elements together.

![People + Culture = Everything](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/people_culture_everything.png?resize=484%2C305)

## Diversity is fundamental to each of these elements

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/diversity_is_fundamental.png?resize=900%2C653)

**Diversity is a critical driver of innovation.** We need diverse inputs for entrepreneurship – from the people who start companies and work for them to the resources they access – and we need a culture that supports heterogeneous communities, a range of industries, and a wealth of different ideas.

In *investing*, a diverse portfolio has been shown to perform best. In *human systems*, diversity is linked to more innovation. In *business*, diverse teams have exhibited stronger results – in their ability to attract, retain, and satisfy employees and to increase shareholder returns.[24](https://www.kauffman.org/ecosystem-playbook-draft-3/glossary-and-resources/endnotes)

Similarly, entrepreneurial ecosystems need diversity to truly thrive.

### A community of entrepreneurs…

**People are at the center of an entrepreneurial ecosystem.** They are its fundamental building blocks. All the ideas, incubators, and capital in the world will not create anything valuable without talented people putting them to good use.

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/ecosystem_builders.png?resize=802%2C432)

### …and the people who support them.

**Entrepreneurship is a community sport.**[25](https://www.kauffman.org/ecosystem-playbook-draft-3/glossary-and-resources/endnotes) Building companies requires people throughout the community to help in a wide variety of roles. They include:

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/people_who_support_entrepreneurs.png?resize=581%2C217)

People may move in and out of the community over time, shift between roles, or even play several roles at the same time.

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/shaking_hands.png?resize=633%2C390)

## Talent that helps companies grow

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/community_garden.png?resize=788%2C431)

**Some of the most successful entrepreneurial ecosystems are also talent magnets and incubators.** Ecosystem building requires attracting, retaining, and cultivating talented people – including entrepreneurs, but also potential employers and other supporting partners. Strong ecosystems tend to have substantial cross-linkages between schools, universities, and the private sector so that talent supply and demand are efficiently matched.

### Knowledge and resources to help entrepreneurs.

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/person_reading.png?resize=480%2C272)

**Strong ecosystems allow entrepreneurs to quickly find knowledge and resources they need to succeed.** The knowledge and resources required by entrepreneurs are diverse. Knowledge may include answers to logistical questions (“How do I get my export license?”); points of friction (“How do I get my team to function better?”); or simple inquiries. A thriving ecosystem is an environment that encourages people to ask these questions and offers an abundance of easy ways for them to find the answers. Knowledge may reside with other entrepreneurs, other people (such as mentors or professionals), or institutions (such as universities, libraries, or accelerators).

Resources are the assets entrepreneurs need, such as capital, potential hires, office space, professional services, or raw materials. In a successful ecosystem, these resources are more abundant and visible.

It is often connections among individuals and institutions that allow knowledge and resources to flow to entrepreneurs quickly.

### Champions and conveners.

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/people_chatting_coffee_shop.png?resize=581%2C303)

**Champions and conveners promote entrepreneurs, organize the ecosystem, and build awareness.** They advocate for local entrepreneurs and their companies, bring them together in collaboration, challenge them to grow, and push everyone forward. They are the catalysts, connectors, cheerleaders, co-creators, and changemakers.

Champions and conveners wear many different hats. They could be the organizer of a Startup Weekend, the reporter covering local entrepreneurs, the lawyer that introduces clients to others, the early adopter who first buys from new businesses or restaurants, or the chamber leader who shines a spotlight on new businesses. Most importantly, they work relentlessly to help entrepreneurs and companies with little direct benefit for themselves.

## Onramps and intersections

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/entrance.png?resize=425%2C397)

### Onramps…

**Onramps make ecosystems more open by creating clear paths for people to join.** They grow networks by bringing in more talent, thus fostering diversity and allowing for serendipitous interactions that lead to new ideas. Healthy ecosystems have visible and welcoming onramps that make the ecosystem easy to access – for anyone, regardless of their background, experience, or ideas. These onramps may come in the form of *events* like 1 Million Cups; *organizations* like accelerators, small business development centers (SBDCS), or career development centers; or *online communities* like websites or social media groups.

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/ideas_serendipity.png?resize=0%2C0)

### …and intersections.

**Collisions between people, ideas, and resources often allow entrepreneurs to find missing pieces of the puzzles they are trying to solve.** Ecosystems must “engineer serendipity” between disparate elements of the network by creating intersections where such collisions can happen.

Intersections may be *institutions*, such as coworking spaces, research parks, or coffee shops that are regularly frequented by entrepreneurs and their supporters. Or they may be *events*, such as pitch competitions, conferences, or meetups. Intersections may also take place *online*, such as through a Slack channel or Twitter hashtag that convenes a virtual community.

## The community’s story

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/startupville.png?resize=566%2C285)

**The collective story that people tell themselves – and the rest of the world – powerfully shapes an ecosystem’s future.** It frames what is possible.

In Silicon Valley, this story is about the generations of legendary entrepreneurs who were misfits determined to disrupt the status quo. This story inspires new aspiring misfits to arrive in the Valley every day. Some communities, however, tell themselves negative stories. They bemoan the native entrepreneurs who left, the brain drain of young people, or the catastrophic failure of a company many years ago that still serves as a cautionary tale. These negative stories become self-fulfilling and inhibit new entrepreneurs.

A community must discuss, define, and disseminate its collective story, and in that process identify its unique strengths, resources, and opportunities. The best community stories are authentic and true.

This collective story also deeply influences a community’s “sense of place” – a dynamic and complex set of factors that develops the lens through which people experience and make meaning of their experience in and with place.[26](https://www.kauffman.org/ecosystem-playbook-draft-3/glossary-and-resources/endnotes) It is through this sense of place that people imagine its future and their role in it.

### A collaborative culture that is rich in social capital.

**Culture makes the ecosystem come alive.** We find culture in a community’s energy, its attitude toward collaboration and competition, and its enthusiasm for entrepreneurship.

An ecosystem culture that is rich in social capital – the networks, norms, and social trust that facilitate coordination and cooperation for mutual benefit[27](https://www.kauffman.org/ecosystem-playbook-draft-3/glossary-and-resources/endnotes) – is like rocket fuel for entrepreneurial growth. An ecosystem will struggle without a culture of collaboration, cooperation, and trust that inspires people to move quickly, help each other, and be open to novel ideas.

A community’s culture can be cultivated, tended, and nurtured. It is by no means static. People engage in culture change naturally – through the conversations they have, the questions they ask, and the behaviors they model.

![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/social_capital.png?resize=900%2C621)
![](https://www.kauffman.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/collaborative_culture.png?resize=1060%2C1434)

## Contents

# Entrepreneurship ecosystem

|  |  |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f2/Edit-clear.svg/40px-Edit-clear.svg.png)

An **entrepreneurial ecosystem** or **entrepreneurship ecosystems** are peculiar systems of interdependent actors and relations directly or indirectly supporting the creation and growth of new ventures.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## The ecosystem metaphor

"Ecosystem" refers to the elements – individuals, organizations or institutions – outside the individual [entrepreneur](/wiki/Entrepreneur "Entrepreneur") that are conducive to, or inhibitive of, the choice of a person to become an entrepreneur, or the probabilities of his or her success following launch. Organizations and individuals representing these elements are referred to as entrepreneurship stakeholders. Stakeholders are any entity that has an interest, actually or potentially, in there being more entrepreneurship in the region. Entrepreneurship stakeholders may include [government](/wiki/Government "Government"), [schools](/wiki/School "School"), [universities](/wiki/University "University"), [private sector](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector"), [family businesses](/wiki/Family_business "Family business"), [investors](/wiki/Investors "Investors"), [banks](/wiki/Bank "Bank"), entrepreneurs, social leaders, [research centers](/wiki/Research_center "Research center"), [military](/wiki/Military "Military"), labor representatives, [students](/wiki/Student "Student"), [lawyers](/wiki/Lawyer "Lawyer"), [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperatives "Cooperatives"), [communes](/wiki/Intentional_community "Intentional community"), [multinationals](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation"), [private foundations](/wiki/Private_foundation "Private foundation"), and [international aid agencies](/wiki/International_aid_agencies "International aid agencies").

In order to explain or create [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainable "Sustainable") entrepreneurship, one isolated element in the ecosystem is rarely sufficient. In regions which have extensive amounts of entrepreneurship, including [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley"), [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) many of the ecosystem elements are strong and typically have evolved in tandem. Similarly, the formation of these ecosystems suggests that governments or societal leaders who want to foster more entrepreneurship as part of economic policy must strengthen several such elements simultaneously. However, recent research shows that government policy is often limited in what it can do to develop entrepreneurial ecosystems.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In July 2010, the [Harvard Business Review](/wiki/Harvard_Business_Review "Harvard Business Review") published an article by [Daniel Isenberg](/wiki/Daniel_Isenberg "Daniel Isenberg"), Professor of Entrepreneurship Practice at [Babson College](/wiki/Babson_College "Babson College"), entitled “How to Start an Entrepreneurial Revolution.”[[6]](#cite_note-6) In this article, Isenberg describes the environment in which entrepreneurship tends to thrive. Drawing from examples from around the world, the article proposes that entrepreneurs are most successful when they have access to the human, financial and professional resources they need, and operate in an environment in which government policies encourage and safeguard entrepreneurs. This network is described as the entrepreneurship ecosystem.

The [Babson College](/wiki/Babson_College "Babson College") Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Project then categorizes this framework into these domains: policy, finance, culture, supports, [human capital](/wiki/Human_capital "Human capital") and markets. Much additional scholarship has reinforced this conceptualization, and Liguori and colleagues developed a measure that has been widely used nationally to assess communities from Tampa to Philadelphia to Chicago, and more.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Several academic researchers have begun to investigate entrepreneurial ecosystems as well. Spigel[[8]](#cite_note-8) suggests that ecosystems require cultural attributes (a culture of entrepreneurship and histories of successful entrepreneurship), social attributes that are accessed through social ties (worker talent, investment capital, social networks, and entrepreneurial mentors) and material attributes grounded in a specific places (government policies, universities, support services, physical infrastructure, and open local markets). Stam[[9]](#cite_note-9) distinguishes between framework conditions of ecosystems (formal institutions, culture, physical infrastructure, and market demand) with systematic conditions of networks, leadership, finance, talent, knowledge, and support services.

Emerging entrepreneurial ecosystems (EEEs) are often evaluated using tangible metrics like new products, patents, and venture capital funding. However, Hannigan et al. (2022)[[10]](#cite_note-10) argue that understanding these ecosystems requires considering [cultural factors](/wiki/Culture "Culture") alongside material ones. They emphasize that cultural elements, such as community engagement and shared values, play a crucial role in the growth and success of EEEs. By incorporating both cultural and material perspectives, policymakers can better design incentives and regulations to foster economic growth and innovation in these ecosystems. This approach suggests that building cultural infrastructure is as important as financial and technical support in developing thriving entrepreneurial environments.

There are several key conditions that typically define a healthy ecosystem. The ecosystem:

## Related content

**Startup ecosystem** – following the financial downturn of 2008 and the long-lasting slow growth period, there have been increasing focus towards fostering more [startup company](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") creation around the world to further target regional support efforts towards those type of companies that have higher innovation, growth and job creation potential. This has also led to an increasing focus on [startup ecosystem](/wiki/Startup_ecosystem "Startup ecosystem") development.

**University-based entrepreneurship ecosystem** – Entrepreneurship thrives in ecosystems in which multiple stakeholders play key roles. Academic institutions are central in shaping young people’s attitudes, skills and behaviours. However, actors outside of the education systems play an increasingly critical role in working with formal and informal educational programmes as well as reaching out to underserved and socially excluded targets groups. This requires collaboration and multistakeholder partnerships. Entrepreneurship ecosystems commonly refer to academic programs within a university that focus on the development of student/graduate entrepreneurs and/or the commercialization of technology or intellectual property developed at the university level.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) However before the entrepreneurial ecosystem can bloom, the education system must embrace the idea that entrepreneurship is a core element of higher education.

Also, entrepreneurship is usually perceived as the cure-all solution for deprivation depletion. Advocates assert that it guides to job design, higher earnings, and lower deprivation prices in the towns within it happens. Others disagree that numerous entrepreneurs are generating low-capacity companies helping regional markets. [[13]](#cite_note-13)

**Business cluster** – A [business cluster](/wiki/Business_cluster "Business cluster") is a geographic concentration of interconnected businesses, suppliers, and associated institutions in a particular field. Early research was done in this context by Benjamin Chinitz in 1961. Chinitz posed several theories, but most importantly noticed a correlation between average firm size and average growth rates within regions.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In addition, Glaeser, Kerr and Ponzetto followed up this research and confirmed the relationship between smaller average firm size and higher growth rates.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Chatterji, Glaeser and Kerr also noted that some of the most famous entrepreneurial clusters (Silicon Valley, Boston's Route 128 Corridor, and Research Triangle Park) were located near large research universities.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Governments often look to clusters to stimulate innovation and entrepreneurship in their region. When clusters are applied to entrepreneurship, experts agree governments should not seek to create new clusters, but rather reinforce existing ones.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Tony Hsieh, founder of Zappos, has begun a project to see if an entrepreneurial cluster can be created in Las Vegas.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

--- SOURCE 4: What an Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Actually Is ---
URL: https://hbr.org/2014/05/what-an-entrepreneurial-ecosystem-actually-is

SUMMARY:
## My Account # What an Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Actually Is Fostering entrepreneurship has become a core component of economic development in cities and countries around the world. The predominant metaphor for fostering entrepreneurship as an economic development strategy is the “entrepreneurship ecosystem.” It should come as no surprise, however, that as any innovative idea spreads, so do the misconceptions and mythology. Here is a quick true-false test that will serve as a reality check on entrepreneurship ecosystems, and on the connection between entrepreneurship and development more generally. FULL CONTENT:
## Explore HBR

# What an Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Actually Is

by [Daniel Isenberg](/search?term=Daniel Isenberg)

![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Twitter.7ac71c2c.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Facebook.f94faecd.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/LinkedIn.c9a4114a.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Save.635e3a71.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/BuyCopy.088ae0cd.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Print.61d005bb.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Twitter.7ac71c2c.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Facebook.f94faecd.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/LinkedIn.c9a4114a.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Save.635e3a71.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/BuyCopy.088ae0cd.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Print.61d005bb.svg)

Fostering entrepreneurship has become a core component of economic development in cities and countries around the world. The predominant metaphor for fostering entrepreneurship as an economic development strategy is the “entrepreneurship ecosystem.” It should come as no surprise, however, that as any innovative idea spreads, so do the misconceptions and mythology. Here is a quick true-false test that will serve as a reality check on entrepreneurship ecosystems, and on the connection between entrepreneurship and development more generally. It’s important to get this right, because the emergence of entrepreneurship as a policy priority has paralleled (and is at least partly in response to) disappointment with dictated industrial policy, barren “[cluster](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cluster_development)” strategies, and the failure of a limited focus on a set of macroeconomic framework conditions (the so-called “[Washington Consensus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Washington_Consensus)“). If we’re to prevent the enthusiasm for entrepreneurial ecosystems from also fizzling out, we need to get a better grip on what the term really means.

![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Twitter.7ac71c2c.svg)
![](/assets-mfe-articles/_next/static/media/Facebook.f94faecd.svg)

URL: https://www.shutterstock.com/shutterstock/photos/1028924953/display_1500/stock-photo-business-illustration-showing-the-concept-of-entrepreneurship-ecosystem-1028924953.jpg
DESCRIPTION: Entrepreneurship ecosystem diagram with icons and arrows connecting them.

URL: https://buildingbridgesforbusiness.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/Entrepreneurship-Ecosystem.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates a cyclical relationship highlighting the roles of funding from governments, the importance of outreach, and partnerships between businesses and entrepreneurial-academic institutions in fostering an entrepreneurial ecosystem.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Entrepreneurship ecosystems are networks of interdependent actors, including individuals and organizations, that support the creation and growth of new ventures, fostering collaboration and resource sharing.
These ecosystems play a crucial role in economic growth by creating jobs, increasing innovation, and generating wealth, contributing to a vibrant and sustainable economy.

Entrepreneurship ecosystems drive innovation by facilitating diverse ideas, promoting collaboration, and providing access to resources and knowledge, essential for developing new products and services.

Key stakeholders include entrepreneurs, investors, educational institutions, government entities, and support organizations, all contributing to a thriving entrepreneurial environment and shared success.
The report aims to analyze ecosystem metrics, assess their impact on entrepreneurship, and recommend strategies for enhancing collaboration among stakeholders to drive growth and innovation.
